data.title: Kanzhun: Staying Bullish On Potential Growth Acceleration And Shareholder Base Expansion
data.description:
I'm still positive on Kanzhun Limited (NASDAQ:BZ) (2076.HK). Its near-term prospects are good, judging by its better-than-anticipated results and guidance. More people should be interested in owning its shares with the change in the company's cash distribution strategy.

My earlier March 12, 2025, article assessed BZ's above-consensus 4Q24 numbers and its favorable outlook over a longer time horizon.

Q2 Bottom Line And Q3 Sales Guide Exceeded Expectations

The firm issued a 6-K filing on Aug. 20, 2025, disclosing its 2Q2025 financials. BZ registered a normalized EPS of CNY 2.09 in the Apr-Jun '25 period. As per S&P Capital IQ data, this was one-tenth higher than the consensus projection. The group's earnings also rose by a third YoY for 2Q2025. Its per-share net profit expansion in the same quarter a year ago was a more modest +25%.

Its recent quarterly revenue increased by a solid 10% year on year. But that was slower than 1Q25's +13% growth. Better margins (as opposed to sales improvement) were the major driver of BZ's Q2 earnings beat. AI-related tailwinds, fixed-cost leverage, and cost-cutting efforts boosted its profitability.

Kanzhun saw its EBIT-to-revenue ratio widen by +746bps (basis points) to 42% in 2Q25. That came in 4.4ppts (source: S&P Capital IQ) above the sell-side's prediction.

At its results briefing, BZ drew attention to "improved operational efficiency as we continue to engage AI in our daily operations." This was reflected in its cost of sales, which declined 3% for the recent three-month period.

Its results call commentary also mentioned the "scale effects" for its "online recruitment marketplace" business and the intent of "keeping cost growth rate lower than revenue growth." This explains why BZ's "Operating Expenses" or "OPEX" contracted by 7% YoY in the second quarter despite realizing higher sales. Separately, the company is anticipating that its topline will go up 12.2% (versus consensus's prior +10.3% estimate) in the 3rd quarter on year-on-year terms.

Morgan Stanley (MS) published a report (not available publicly) titled "Kanzhun: Chart Of the Day" on Monday Aug. 18, 2025. MS cited recent "Sensor Tower" data which indicated BZ's gross revenue on Apple's (AAPL) App Store grew 19% YoY (vs 2Q's +9%) in the third quarter this far.

Kanzhun's top-line performance was previously hurt by an imbalance between the Mainland Chinese employment market's demand & supply. This has become less of a problem. BZ shared at its analyst call that the PRC's "newly added verified graduates" dropped "over 20%" in Jun-Jul '25, while the nation's "new job openings for fresh graduates grew by over 18%" in the same time frame.

BZ's sales growth has moderated for six quarters in a row since 1Q24, notwithstanding the latest bottom line beat. A potential reversal of this negative trend implied by the management forecast (Q3's +12% guide vis-a-vis Q2's actual +10%) could act as the stock's re-rating trigger.

Stockholder Base Could Be Broadened With Updated Capital Management Approach

If you were searching for income plays, Kanzhun wouldn't have qualified as a candidate in the past. But many investors might be reconsidering their views now. In tandem with its results announcement, BZ also disclosed a formal cash distribution framework and additional repurchases.

Before this latest development, the firm had only made a one-off payment of $0.18/share to shareholders in Dec '23 with no promise of future payouts. Assuming that I was an income-focused investor, it was unlikely that this stock would be on my watchlist.

Looking ahead, BZ has guided that its stockholders will receive recurring dividends on a yearly basis. In the current year, its intention is to allocate $0.08 billion for this purpose. The company also enlarged its existing buyback plan, expiring in late-Aug next year, by +67% to $0.25 billion.

BZ's forward shareholder yield, calculated as "sum of dividends and buybacks divided by market capitalization," is estimated to be a decent 3% based on the figures presented by management.

Besides attracting a new group of yield-chasing investors, this new capital allocation stance should help narrow any valuation discount associated with a "lazy balance sheet." Its current net cash-to-market cap is 20%, which seems excessive.

Variant Views You Shouldn't Ignore

Kanzhun's future results could be below expectations in the event of a severe economic slowdown in China. In that scenario, corporations pull back on hiring, and there is an oversupply of job seekers.

The firm might not complete the repurchase program. This can happen if it decides to re-allocate capital to investments or continue hoarding cash.

My Closing Remarks

I've decided to stick with a "Buy" rating for this name.

In my March '25 write-up, I highlighted that Kanzhun was trading at a lower valuation vis-a-vis listed comparables like "Recruit Holdings (OTCPK:RCRRF) [6098:JP] and SEEK Limited (OTCPK:SKLTF) [SEK:AU]."

BZ continues to be attractively priced at a forward earnings multiple of 20x, according to S&P Capital IQ. RCRRF and SKLTF currently boast P/Es of 31x and 43x, respectively. Kanzhun's potential upside is +55% (31/20), if its P/E can re-rate to be on par with Recruit Holdings. The stock's catalysts include a more rapid pace of growth in 3Q25 and a broader shareholder base.

data.title: VXX: A Behind The Scenes Look Inside The Volatility Index ETN
data.description:
Thesis

Volatility (or 'vol') as an asset class is a lesser known concept by many investors, and in finance it refers to the degree of variation in the price of a financial instrument. Volatility is not directional, and it is a statistical measure that indicates how much the price of an asset fluctuates. A higher volatility means the price can move dramatically in either direction, representing a higher degree of risk. Conversely, an asset with low volatility has a more stable and predictable price.

In the past ten years the markets have evolved towards allowing retail investors to access this niche asset class via various exchange listed securities. While large institutional players have had the opportunity to gain exposure to vol for a very long time via over the counter ('OTC') volatility swaps and other similar products, retail investors could only use options trading for long periods of time to access volatility exposure.

In today's article we are going to have a closer look at an exchange listed product that gives investors access to volatility, namely the iPath Series B S&P 500® VIX Short-Term Futures™ ETN (BATS:VXX). We are going to look at the fund structure and analytics, as well as get behind the concept of the VIX index and its practical application. This article is part of an educational series, offering Seeking Alpha readers the opportunity to get behind the financial engineering and theoretical reasoning that drives the creation of such a fund.

A primer on volatility

Before we dive into the structure for VXX, let us remember the ETN is designed to offer exposure to the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index Total Return (the "Index"). The Index in turn is designed to provide access to equity market volatility through CBOE Volatility Index (the "VIX Index") futures.

While market participants have probably heard of the VIX before, many readers might not be entirely familiar with what it actually does, and how it is composed. Let us take a simple example that presents the VIX in an easy-to-understand light: let us say we have an equity market that goes up or down by more than 2% daily, and ends up after a month at +6%; similarly we have another market that goes up every day by +0.3% and ends up after a month at +6%. Without knowing anything about volatility or options, which one would you say is less volatile? And more importantly, if you were to sell a call option, which of the two markets would present a more attractive opportunity?

As stated in the thesis, markets with low volatility are more predictable, thus an investor can have a better comfort with the ultimate goal for the price action. When uncertainty is high, option prices are going to reflect that, and options will be more expensive because market participants simply do not know where the market will end up in 30 days. In our theoretical example the market with +/- 2% every day is more volatile, and options on that market will be more expensive. Which brings us to the VIX building methodology.

The VIX is calculated using the real-time prices of S&P 500 index options. It is not based on the stock prices of the S&P 500 itself. The VIX is essentially a weighted average of the implied volatilities for both calls and puts on the S&P 500 at various strikes and with maturities navigating around the 30-day mark. An investor can access a very good white paper on the VIX composition here. The methodology suffered a big change in 2003, when VIX starting using a wide range of strikes, whereas before only at-the-money strike prices were used.

Another very useful "rule of thumb" for the VIX is that an investor can divide the VIX by 16 to get the daily expected move for the S&P 500. So if the VIX is 16, the expected daily move in the S&P 500 is 1%. If the VIX is 25, the expected daily move is 1.56%.

Composition of the iPath Series B S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures ETN

The fund contains the two front month VIX futures contracts:

We can see the name having a roughly 90% position in September futures, and a 10% position in October futures. The ETN therefore is a take on VXX futures, rather than the VIX index itself. There is a reason for that.

VIX futures are less volatile than spot VIX, thus providing for a smoother profile for the fund. We can see that during the April 2025 VIX blow-out which saw the VIX go above 50, yet front month VIX futures barely broke 30:

VIX futures move less because the VIX itself cannot stay very high for very long. Remind yourselves that the VIX represents the implied volatilities from options. When there is fear, people buy puts, thus driving up implied volatility. When people buy less puts, implied volatility comes down. The VIX cannot stay very high (i.e. above 35) for a long period because investors would need to buy an enormous amount of protection daily in order to sustain that high implied vol.

Without needing to go more into the technical aspects here, just remember that VIX futures are less volatile, and the current fund build with two front month futures ensures a smoother move in VXX over longer periods of time. Investors can access the VIX futures ladder here, on the CBOE website.

VXX Is Not a Buy and Hold Name

Investors need to keep in mind that VXX is not a buy and hold name due to its futures utilization. Every time a financial product uses futures, an investor needs to understand if the 'roll' effect will be beneficial or not. Futures utilization means the fund will always need to buy the next contract when the held one expires. That can translate into gains or losses depending on what the curve looks like. Let us have a look:

The above is the current VIX futures curve and it is upwards sloping. This is the normal shape of the curve and it is called contango. When the futures curve is in contango, a long fund like VXX is going to lose money on the roll. We have summarized the various scenarios below:

So in a normal market VXX will always lose money on the roll, thus having a negative long term effect on the fund. We can see that via the price move in the past two years:

Since January 2024 VXX is down -36%, despite the massive vol blowout in April 2025. This is mainly due to the roll effect. Investors therefore need to understand VXX is not a buy and hold investment, but a fund you own for short periods of time as a portfolio hedge, or a name that people can day trade.

ETN vs ETF - be aware of the additional risk

Unlike exchange traded funds which are bankruptcy remote, ETNs are actually bonds issued by a financial institution, in this case Barclays. As per their own disclosures:

The ETNs are riskier than ordinary unsecured debt securities and have no principal protection. The ETNs are unsecured debt obligations of the issuer, Barclays Bank PLC, and are not, either directly or indirectly, an obligation of or guaranteed by any third party. Any payment to be made on the ETNs, including any payment at maturity or upon redemption, depends on the ability of Barclays Bank PLC to satisfy its obligations as they come due. An investment in the ETNs involves significant risks, including possible loss of principal and may not be suitable for all investors.

An investor buying VXX is also underwriting Barclays as an ongoing concern. If Barclays goes under, irrespective what volatility does, VXX might incur a very significant loss from the company's bankruptcy, with VXX holders becoming creditors to the estate. It is important to understand this aspect because when faced a choice between an ETN and an ETF, investors should always chose the ETF from a structural standpoint.

Some analytics

  • AUM: $737 million
  • Expense ratio: 0.89%
  • Standard deviation: 52%
  • Average daily dollar volume (3m): $233 mm

Key Advantages and Disadvantages

Advantages:

  • The most liquid and traded vol fund
  • The ETN has an options chain, which investors can use to gain exposure to the fund with limited downside (i.e., buying calls rather than buying the fund outright)
  • Accessible way to trade vol without the OTC market or complex options strategies
  • A simple way to introduce a temporary portfolio hedge

Disadvantages:

  • Not a buy-and-hold instrument
  • Negative roll effect in a normalized market (i.e., futures curve in contango)
  • Barclays default risk via the ETN structure

Conclusion

VXX is an exchange traded note from Barclays. The ETN contains the front months VIX futures contracts, and represents a long play on volatility. The VIX is a measure of implied volatility for various calls and puts on the S&P 500 with a rough 30-day maturity. The measure serves as a way to quantify expected S&P 500 moves and has become an asset class of its own in the past two decades. Retail investors can go long volatility via VXX, but have to be mindful that the ETN is not a buy and hold instrument due to the negative roll effect in normalized markets.

This article answers these three main questions about VXX:

  • VXX is an ETN, what is the difference between ETNs and ETFs?
  • Is VXX meant for long term investing or short-term trading?
  • How can investors use VXX most effectively?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Beyond Is Trying On A New Name, But With The Same Aimless Strategy
data.description:
Back in May, when I last covered Beyond (NYSE:BYON), I was critical of their seemingly incoherent strategy, focusing in particular on their buying of Zulily as a game changing acquisition and then selling it off again shortly thereafter. I rated them a sell, though the market has clearly had a differing opinion.

The gains were fueled mostly around a talking point that they were looking to monetize their blockchain assets. Blockchain is one of the words that people love to throw around, because it makes it sound like Beyond is deep in the cryptocurrency world, even though in practice it’s a relatively small fraction of their overall business operations.

Then the other news dropped. Beyond, which less than two years ago was known as Overstock, will be changing its name yet again, this time declaring itself Bed Bath & Beyond, and planning to retake the old ticker symbol of BBBY later this month.

So a lot has happened, at least on the surface, and it’s high time we took another look at Bed Bath & Beyond né Beyond né Overstock, the e-tailer that is chasing headlines right now with a new sobriquet.

Beyond's Revenue Shrinks, Losses Continue

Let’s start with earnings, because in the end, that’s still what matters at the end of the day. Beyond has been pushing to cut their large losses by limiting the number of nonprofitable SKUs they offer on their websites. That’s allowed them to shrink the size of losses, but at the expense of revenue.

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

We can see where Beyond was able to somewhat improve their gross margins, and somewhat shrink their operating losses, likely attributable to scaling back on the nonprofitable SKUs. At the same time, losses endure, and they have a combination I absolutely hate to see, shrinking revenue and a significant increase in outstanding shares.

Beyond does maintain an acceptable amount of working cash at present ($121 million per the 10-Q), though their current ratio is slipping below 1.0 and without profitability, that cash is only going to sustain them for so long.

Persistent quarterly losses (they haven’t had a profitable quarter since Q2 of 2022) and declining revenue seem particularly unappealing when combined with the dilutive effect that issuing yet more shares is going to have on investments.

Beyond presented themselves, in the conference call at least, as having a plan that includes leaning into Overstock.com’s historic rug business and aiming to expand their fine watch category on the site, though they conceded those watches will likely lead to lower margins in the future.

Forgive me for saying, but those sound like the plans of Overstock.com in 2010 or so, not the plans of Beyond in 2025, and yet here we are, with the company seemingly hoping to recapture its former glory as a e-commerce also-ran by taking their old business model, slapping a fresh coat of paint on it, and hoping nobody notices.

Whether that is going to return them to break-even or not in 2025, when luxury watches aren’t exactly flying off the shelves, remains to be seen. CEO Marcus Lemonis suggested in the conference call that Beyond was expected to be a “massive winner” when the housing market stabilizes. Putting aside the question of what massive winner means in the context of this relatively small company, recent news suggests if anything, the US housing market is slumping even worse of late.

If that’s the environment in which Beyond would thrive, they’re still positioned to struggle into the future. Estimates suggest analysts see it that way too, with losses of 40¢ and 44¢ expected in the next two quarters.

tZERO: Not Nothing But Value Likely Overstated

As I mentioned above, much of the recent surge in Beyond’s price is predicated on the idea that they’re going to take their blockchain assets, notably tZERO, public as a way to unlock value to shareholders. It sounded very exciting and investors were quick to buy into Beyond stock to try to get in on this. Crypto’s exciting, after all.

Yet when one looks at tZERO and what it really boils down to, it seems much less compelling. They are envisioned as a trading platform using the blockchains to handle trades in securities. It would compete with the NASDAQ, at least in theory. In practice the platform is sparsely used, and then for tokenizing Beyond’s own assets as preferred stock offerings, and a few miniscule outfits trying to raise capital without going to the effort that a proper IPO on a real exchange would require.

What this market could plausibly be worthy as a publicly traded company is anyone’s guess, and quite frankly if tZERO was all it was cracked up to be, one would think they’d be going public straight on their own platform instead of reporting that they’re seeking a direct listing on the New York Stock Exchange.

No Store in California: Beyond Chases Headlines

Then we have Beyond, which again just named itself that at the tail end of 2023, announcing another new name, Bed Bath & Beyond, and taking that old company’s ticker symbol, BBBY, under which it will begin trading starting on August 29.

I was rather fond of the old Bed Bath & Beyond a couple decades ago, but one should make no mistake this isn’t a dramatic shift in direction for Beyond, it’s just a new name, and a new opportunity to parlay that familiarity into some media coverage.

And Lemonis wasted no time doing so, announcing earlier this week that because of its business climate, Bed Bath & Beyond will not open any stores in California. Media outlets latched on to this story, and people on Twitter were quick to embrace the idea as vindication for their political positions.

Just so we’re clear though, Bed Bath & Beyond isn’t opening any stores anywhere else either. They’re an e-tailer, so announcing their shunning America’s largest state for brick and mortar business is a rather empty flex. There is exactly one Bed Bath & Beyond business on the entire planet right now, and Beyond doesn’t even operate that one.

That one store is in metro Nashville, Tennessee, and is operated by The Brand House Collective (TBHC), formerly Kirkland’s Inc. Beyond licensed them the right to use the name, and Beyond does own a substantial minority stake in TBHC, though recent earnings also show them in a similar state of declining revenue and persistent losses in recent years.

TBHC intends to ultimately rebrand some more of its stores as Bed Bath & Beyond, though in practice it’s not the same as the former company, which went bankrupt in 2023, before Beyond bought the rights to the name.

Beyond announcing they’re rebuking California made for a novel headline, but in practice they were never going to open retail stores there. In the conference call just three weeks ago, Lemonis said that Beyond’s “core competency is not to operate retail locations, not to spend our shareholders' cash on CapEx growth and things of that nature.” Even if Beyond had the working capital to try to open a retail location, which to be clear they likely don’t, they never had any intention of doing so in the first place. TBHC, for its part, seems content to bleed cash while they try to rebrand more of their own stores as Bed Bath & Beyond as well, because the name is seen as holding some currency.

Whether renaming the stores will help TBHC is anyone’s guess, but the odds don’t look great, and TBHC does, for the record, already operate no less than 14 stores inside California.

What Could Go Right?

Beyond seems to be leaning into meme publicity rather than active profit, and that might continue to work for a time. I don’t totally rule out that the market will continue to reward Beyond for chasing headlines and maybe even substantially overvalue tZERO if it does get its day in the sun on the New York Stock Exchange.

My underlying thesis on this investment is that Beyond is unprofitable, and likely will remain so for the foreseeable future. That doesn’t preclude the market continue to trade up the price of the stock for a time, as heaven only knows we’ve seen that happen before.

The only way I envision the company ever getting to profitability is if it turns out they really are positioned to take advantage of a stabilized housing market and, against all suggestions from analysts, we actually get a stabilized housing market some time in the near future. Failing that, I just don’t see where they’re at all viable on their more rugs and watches business model.

Don’t Invest on Hope

While conceding that the market does wrongly price some stocks and it’s entirely possible they’re going to do that even moreso with Beyond in the future. I would urge investors though to invest on the basis of facts and earnings, not hope being sold to us like so many discount rugs.

I’m going to maintain my sell rating on Beyond, leaning closer to strong sell because I feel like the increased number of shares and the persistent unprofitability are a dangerous combination and if that continues, there’s going to be even less to like here.

People who are long Beyond should keep a close eye both on the revenue and the number of shares outstanding in any given quarter, because if they don’t stem the tide of revenue decrease, no amount of improved margins is going to warrant their current market cap.

data.title: Still Compounding: India's Structural Momentum Amid Market Noise
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

The Indian equity market in 2025 hasn't quite captured headlines like China's tactical rebound or the broader emerging markets resurgence. On a relative basis, India looks like it's taking a breather. But under the surface, it's anything but stagnant. From a fundamental investor's lens, this is precisely when narratives get dislocated from reality—and when forward-looking capital should be sharpening its focus.

Think of what's happening as a shift, not a stall. The National Stock Exchange Fifty Index is up roughly 3% year-to-date, a modest showing in comparison to benchmarks like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, up closer to 20% over the same period.1 This isn't a time to lose confidence in the India story—it's a time to adjust expectations with the long-term in mind. Beneath the index-level quiet, capital markets are alive. India is pacing toward a record IPO year, with more than $6.7 billion raised so far—surpassing even the same period last year. Domestic appetite is deep, and post-IPO performance has been encouraging.2 This isn't speculative froth; it's institutionalization in motion.

Looking to figure 1:

  • The Best Performers Are the Least Represented:In 2025, for the first half of the year, the top-performing emerging markets—like Greece (+83.05%), Poland (+59.97%) and Colombia (+71.44%)—carried very little exposure in the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, highlighting a sharp disconnect between performance and representation.
  • The Biggest Market Exposures Are Lagging: Despite comprising more than 80% of the MSCI EM Index weight, the top five markets by weight (China, Taiwan, India, South Korea, Brazil) have significantly underperformed some of the aforementioned smaller exposures.
  • A Tough Relative Period for India's Equities? Investors anchored to India's long-term fundamentals may feel whiplashed in 2025—seeing a little more than a 1% return, while the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, powered by peripheral outperformers, surged ahead. But that discomfort stems not from flawed conviction, but from a benchmark temporarily disconnected from the structural growth engine they actually own.

Figure 1: The Disconnect: 2025's Top EM Performers Hold Little Index Weight

Source: MSCI. Performance is YTD 2025, through 8/18/25. Weight is as of 7/31/25. Any market labeled "no" has a weight less than 4.06%, Brazil's weight as the fifth-largest market exposure in the Index. Bold type notes weight within the top five market exposures of the MSCI Emerging Markets Index as of 7/31/25. Subject to change.

India's Household Balance Sheets Are Evolving—Fast

There's a quiet revolution underway in how Indian households allocate capital. We talk often about the U.S. 401(k) moment—India is building its own version. With more than $42 billion in year-to-date domestic fund inflows dwarfing $8 billion in foreign outflows, the marginal buyer of Indian equities is no longer an offshore account manager in Singapore or London—it's a domestic retail investor with a growing systematic investment plan (SIP) account,3 tax incentives and mobile-first access to capital markets.4

This shift matters. It builds resilience. It creates a funding base that doesn't get spooked by short-term geopolitics or a bad U.S. consumer price index (CPI) print. And more importantly, it lays the foundation for long-term valuation support. Price-to-earnings (P/E) multiples across discretionary and retail sectors are running below historical averages—even with robust return on equity (ROE) and earnings growth outlooks.5

GST Reform as a Market Catalyst

India's macro toolkit is hitting stride. A decade ago, CPI inflation and oil imports would have dictated the market's every move. Today, the Reserve Bank of India has defanged volatility through inflation targeting and policy credibility. The result? A smoother glidepath for interest rates and, by extension, market multiples. Fiscal prudence hasn't meant austerity—it's meant a redirection toward capex, infrastructure and productivity.

The next major shift is likely to come through Goods and Services Tax (GST) reform. Nearly a decade after its introduction, policy makers are preparing the system's next iteration—simplifying rate structures and reducing today's patchwork of slabs into a more streamlined two- or three-tier model. For companies, this means lower compliance costs, greater clarity in planning and a more predictable investment environment. For consumers, it translates into fewer distortions across categories and a smoother national marketplace.

Equally important is the rise of digital integration and enforcement. The government has been piloting real-time invoice matching, e-way bill harmonization and AI-driven fraud detection. These aren't just efficiency upgrades—they expand the formal economy, improve tax buoyancy without raising rates and strengthen the state's fiscal resilience.

Markets Don't Always Reward Strength Immediately

2025 has been a reminder: market narratives often lag fundamentals. For patient capital, that's an opportunity, not a frustration. India is now contributing one-fifth of global working-age population growth and could account for a similar share of incremental global gross domestic product (GDP) by 2035.6 Markets may get temporarily distracted by what's happening in AI trade rotations or Chinese property rescue packages, but the underlying compounding in India is still happening—with increasing clarity.

The investment thesis here doesn't hinge on this quarter's PMI print or the timing of the next Fed cut. It hinges on a regime shift: from external dependence to internal strength, from volatility to durability and from promise to scale.

Conclusion: A Strategy More Aligned with India's Long-Term Equity Potential

Against this backdrop of temporary performance dislocation and index concentration, investors need to ask not just where they are invested in India—but how. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index remains heavily skewed toward mega-cap names and countries with large weightings but uneven fundamentals. Even within India itself, many index-linked strategies are top-heavy, overexposed to high-P/E growth narratives and reliant on a narrow group of market leaders. This leaves portfolios vulnerable to valuation shocks and style rotations. In contrast, India's real economic dynamism—especially in 2025—is increasingly powered by mid-sized firms across industrials, financials, consumer cyclicals and technology services, many of which are consistently profitable but underrepresented in capitalization-weighted benchmarks.

The WisdomTree India Earnings Fund (EPI) offers a structurally differentiated solution. Designed to track the total return performance of the WisdomTree India Earnings Index, the strategy deliberately screens for profitability, including only companies that generate positive earnings from core operations. This earnings-weighted methodology naturally tilts exposure away from speculative high-P/E names and allocates capital based on real business performance—not market cap alone. As a result, EPI has historically delivered a more balanced blend of large-, mid- and small-cap Indian equities, capturing the depth of India's economic base while mitigating valuation risk. In a year like 2025—where headline returns don't tell the full story—this approach becomes particularly powerful.

Figure 2: Standardized Performance

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 7/19/25, with returns as of 6/30/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performances and to download the respective Fund prospectuses, click here.

It's important to recognize that China is the single largest market within the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, accounting for more than 29% of the benchmark's weight. This dominant position means that even modest outperformance by Chinese equities can significantly influence the total return of the MSCI EM Index—especially when countries like India, with a nearly 17% weight, are appreciating at a slower pace. In 2025, we've seen precisely this dynamic at play: China has staged a tactical rebound from depressed levels, driven by a mix of policy support, improved investor sentiment and a bounce in select tech and consumer sectors.7 Meanwhile, India's performance, though solid in absolute terms, appears more muted in comparison. For investors benchmarking to MSCI EM, this can create a false sense of missed opportunity—as if India is falling behind, when in fact, the composition of the benchmark is doing much of the performance lifting.

But that short-term framing misses the deeper, more durable trend. As is visible in figure 3, over a 3-, 5- and 10-year horizon, India has consistently outperformed China on both equity returns and earnings growth metrics. India's structural drivers—demographics, macro stability, domestic consumption and a reform-oriented policy regime—have created a fertile environment for long-term capital compounding. In contrast, China faces persistent headwinds from demographic decline, real estate overhangs and a slower transition to consumption-led growth. So, while China may look like the performance leader in select quarters or even years, it has done so from a lower base and with greater volatility. For investors focused on sustained exposure to emerging market growth, India's long-term leadership remains the more resilient,especially when paired with strategies that move beyond simple market-cap exposure.

Figure 3: India's Near-Term Underperformance andLonger-Term Outperformance

Sources: WisdomTree, MSCI, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 8/19/25, with returns as of 8/18/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. The performance data quoted represents past performance and is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performances and to download the respective Fund prospectuses, click here.

If Prime Minister Modi is successful at creating potentially positive catalysts, like GST reforms, as one example, it could unlock what we view as the underlying strength of India's market—a strength that we have not really seen for much of 2025.

2 Source: M. Mishra, "India on track for record IPO year as equity market booms," Financial Times, 7/10/25.

3 A systematic investment plan is a method or mechanism for investing in mutual funds—specifically designed for retail investors to contribute fixed amounts at regular intervals (e.g., monthly or quarterly) into selected mutual fund schemes.

4 Source: Mishra, 2025.

5 Source: A. Menon, S. Rathi, S. Vohra and G. Varma, "India Consumer Discretionary" [investor presentation], Morgan Stanley India Company Private Limited, 7/17/25.

7 Source: W. Sandlund and C. Kay, "Chinese equities rebound on policy boost and tech recovery," Financial Times, 7/8/25.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.
