data.title: Caution
data.description:
The word of today is "Caution."

At the top of my current strategy, of receiving income, is one word: “Caution.” We are currently faced with several serious issues, including tariffs, two wars, the politicizations of our judicial system, our upcoming midterm elections, and the choice of many of our politicians to put politics above what is good for the country. The America of olden days has been replaced, and we are suffering the consequences in the economy as well as in the markets.

The Federal Reserve minutes highlight a split over effects of Trump’s tariffs on inflation. Some members of the policy setting board noted that it is inappropriate to wait for clarity before cutting rates. Some are about-faced from this notion, and the tensions in the country and in our markets are quite high, in my opinion.

Some U.S. rate setters are backing away from the Federal Reserve’s reluctance to cut borrowing costs, even though many were still worried that Donald Trump’s tariffs will worsen inflation. This was made clear recently in the minutes of the Fed’s July meeting. An account of the Fed’s July 29-30 policy deliberations showed that, while some rate setters still wanted to see how economic data develops in the coming months, others thought they may need to make a call on whether or not to lower borrowing costs sooner than that.

The central bank has kept its benchmark federal funds target range on hold at 4.25-4.50 per cent this year, amid concerns that tariffs imposed by the White House on U.S. imports could stoke another wave of inflation. The minutes of the rate setting Federal Open Market Committee, published on Wednesday, showed that some members noted that "it would not be feasible or appropriate to wait for complete clarity on the tariffs’ effects on inflation before adjusting the stance of monetary policy".

Still, the minutes noted that "a majority of participants judged the upside risk to inflation" was larger than that posed by a potential slowdown in the labor market. The minutes said "a few" FOMC members thought tariffs were likely to "lead only to a one-time increase in the price level," rather than a more persistent increase in inflation.

This meeting came before the July jobs report, which showed a sharp slowdown in hiring during the summer, and separate data pointing to a sharp rise in producer prices. The July meeting saw the vote split for the first time this year. Their dissent marked the first time two governors had disagreed with the Federal Reserve’s chairman since 1993.

At the top of the list to succeed Chairman Powell are Waller and Bowman. They have appeared on a Treasury long list to succeed chair Jay Powell when his term expires in May 2026. Markets are broadly expecting a quarter-point cut in interest rates at the September vote, with those expectations rising after the July jobs report. Rate expectations were little changed after the release of the FOMC minutes, with traders anticipating a roughly 85 per cent chance the central bank reduces borrowing costs next month, according to the Financial Times.

“A system of capitalism presumes sound money, not fiat money manipulated by a central bank. Capitalism cherishes voluntary contracts and interest rates that are determined by savings, not credit creation by a central bank.”

- Ron Paul

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Global Industrial Company: Just A Mediocre Play
data.description:
A little over two years ago, in August 2023, one company that I decided to downgrade was Global Industrial Company (NYSE:GIC). Previously, I had the firm rated a "Buy." But because of changes in fundamental health, I believed that downgrading it to a "Hold" was appropriate. Looking back, I should have downgraded it further to a "Sell." I say this because, since then, the stock is up only 16% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 42.7%. Even though revenue for the business continues to climb, bottom-line results have worsened. This should be fine if shares of the firm were trading on the cheap. But based on the data provided, the stock looks mostly fairly valued on both an absolute basis and relative to other comparable firms. Given the current situation we have now, with bottom-line results finally starting to improve, I think it's too late to consider a further downgrade. Instead, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice.

Better opportunities exist

Whenever it has been a while since I have dug into a firm, I'd like to revisit its core operations. This makes it easier to digest changes in fundamental performance from the time that I looked at it previously. It's also beneficial for you, the reader, because it provides details that you may or may not be aware of. When it comes to Global Industrial Company, it would be best to describe the company as a value-added national distributor of industrial equipment and supplies that operates throughout North America. By working with what it calls "relationship marketers" and using its own system of branded e-commerce websites, it sells a wide array of industrial, as well as maintenance, repair, and operation products. Specific examples here include HVAC products and fans, work benches, janitorial and facility maintenance products, carts and trucks, safety and security products, storage and shelving products, and more.

This is the kind of company that strikes at the heart of the North American economy. Almost everything industrial requires the kinds of products that it makes available. However, recently, financial performance has been disappointing. Take the 2023–2024 fiscal years as an example. On the positive side of things, revenue for the company grew 3.3% from $1.27 billion to $1.32 billion. However, this sales increase is a bit deceptive. First, the company had three extra selling days in 2024 compared to 2023. In addition to this, it acquired a company called Indoff in May 2023 in a deal valued at $72.6 million. That acquisition was aimed at increasing its maintenance, repair, and operations presence in North America. Focusing only on average daily sales, the company saw a decline year over year of 0.5%.

When it comes to the bottom line results, the picture worsened. Net income contracted from $70.7 million in 2023 to $60.7 million in 2024. This was even though the company's gross profit margin actually inched up from 34.2% to 34.3%. The bottom line pain that the company saw was driven by a rise in selling, distribution, and administrative costs that grew from $339.3 million to $371.5 million. Some of this included increased marketing spending. But it also included additional costs, such as those associated with Indoff. Soft customer demand in the second half of the year, as well as changes that the company made in 2023 that temporarily changed freight costs, ended up causing problems as well.

Other profitability metrics for the company worsened also. Operating cash flow, for instance, fell from $112 million to $50.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a much smaller decline from $85.3 million to $72.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business pulled back from $102.9 million to $88.1 million. Naturally, this is all negative. But the good news is that we have seen some improvements this year.

For the first half of 2025, revenue for the company totaled $679.9 million. That's an increase of 1.3% compared to the $671.2 million the business generated one year earlier, even though the firm saw weakness in Canada. On the bottom line, results also improved, with net income climbing from $33.3 million to $38.6 million. A lot of this improvement came not only from the increase in revenue, but also from an improvement in the firm's gross profit margin from 34.8% last year to 36% this year. This was because of increased margins associated with its freight and logistics operations, and quality initiatives the company made to reduce freight claims and customer returns.

As was the case with the net profits the company generated, other profitability metrics improved year over year without exception. According to the data provided, operating cash flow popped from $25 million to $35.1 million. On an adjusted basis, the rise was from $39.5 million to $46.4 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the company grew from $47.6 million to $55.5 million.

When it comes to the rest of the year, management has not provided any real guidance. They did say, however, that they are working on boosting profit margins. This comes at a time when, in response to tariffs, which haven't really even significantly impacted the business yet, they will be increasing prices on their goods while simultaneously taking other mitigation actions such as diversifying their supply chain. This will be needed because the firm did mention that it expects headwinds in terms of their margins in the third quarter of this year. In fact, management did say that they believe that the margins experienced in the second quarter of the year will likely not be sustainable. So if anything, profitability might decline relative to revenue as the year goes on.

Even if we assume that the first half of this year is indicative of the second half of this year on a year-over-year basis, shares of the business are not particularly cheap. Using this approach, we would expect net profits for 2025 of $70.4 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $85.4 million, and EBITDA of $102.7 million. Using these estimates and also using the historical results that the company achieved for last year, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These are multiples that align much more similarly to what I would consider to be a fair value prospect. In the table below, I then compared the company to five other similar enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of these companies was cheaper. But on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, four of the five were cheaper. And on an EV to EBITDA basis, three were cheaper, and another was tied with it.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Global Industrial Company 19.0 15.7 12.5
Custom Truck One Source (CTOS) 43.5 5.5 10.2
Distribution Solutions Group (DSGR) 361.2 26.9 12.6
MRC Global (MRC) 38.8 8.2 12.5
Dnow (DNOW) 19.3 7.0 8.0
Willis Lease Finance Corporation (WLFC) 9.2 3.6 10.1

Takeaway

At this time, I really don't see any reason to be terribly optimistic about Global Industrial Company. The recent improvements in profitability are encouraging, but they may not be sustainable, according to management. We have tariff headwinds and the prospect of further economic weakening. If the stock was cheap, I would be more optimistic. But as things stand, shares look pretty solidly fairly valued. Because of this, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice.

data.title: What Powell Did And Didn't Say At Jackson Hole
data.description:
Introduction

The market and financial media were in standby all week waiting for the Jackson Hole Economic Symposium put on by the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, where Fed Chair Jerome Powell would speak. There is no Federal Open Market Committee ("FOMC") meeting this month, so this was the next best thing investors could look toward for monetary policy guidance.

Let's take a look at the speech itself and decipher what Powell said, what he may have meant, and what he didn't say, which made markets jump.

Powell's Jackson Hole Speech

The symposium happens once a year, and so Powell starts off his speech discussing where the Fed was last year.

When I appeared at this podium one year ago, the economy was at an inflection point. Our policy rate had stood at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent for more than a year. That restrictive policy stance was appropriate to help bring down inflation and to foster a sustainable balance between aggregate demand and supply. Inflation had moved much closer to our objective, and the labor market had cooled from its formerly overheated state. Upside risks to inflation had diminished. But the unemployment rate had increased by almost a full percentage point, a development that historically has not occurred outside of recessions. Over the subsequent three [FOMC]...meetings, we recalibrated our policy stance, setting the stage for the labor market to remain in balance near maximum employment over the past year...

The first reference is to the "hawkish cuts" as I've been calling them, signaled by the larger cut and then several smaller cuts before holding off and waiting through 2025 to see if they were having and effect.

The effect has been that unemployment levels, around which 4% is considered "natural," have not meaningfully moved in a year. Neither has inflation, which is slightly elevated compared to last year.

The tone and wording here indicated that the Fed considered these levels positive. While inflation is still running above target, there has been a lot of talk about whether a 2% target is still realistic, and whether the labor market can meaningfully improve from here.

Powell then gets into the challenges, which he sees as largely coming from fiscal and social political policy. He makes a clear distinction here between structural and cyclical changes to the economy, and reiterates a point about how one-time changes to economic conditions don't warrant Fed intervention.

This year, the economy has faced new challenges. Significantly higher tariffs across our trading partners are remaking the global trading system. Tighter immigration policy has led to an abrupt slowdown in labor force growth. Over the longer run, changes in tax, spending, and regulatory policies may also have important implications for economic growth and productivity. There is significant uncertainty about where all of these polices will eventually settle and what their lasting effects on the economy will be.

Changes in trade and immigration policies are affecting both demand and supply. In this environment, distinguishing cyclical developments from trend, or structural, developments is difficult. This distinction is critical because monetary policy can work to stabilize cyclical fluctuations but can do little to alter structural changes.

Powell's Economic Outlook

Because the FOMC operates by committee, Powell can't just go pull a level and change rates month-to-month, but his views on the economy are key to follow to understand where the FOMC will go because he still has an outsized influence not only as a voting member but as the Chair of both the Fed system and the FOMC in particular.

Next in his speech, he talks about the "big three," or labor conditions, gross domestic product ("GDP"), and inflation, starting with labor:

The labor market is a case in point. The July employment report released earlier this month showed that payroll job growth slowed to an average pace of only 35,000 per month over the past three months, down from 168,000 per month during 2024...This slowdown is much larger than assessed just a month ago, as the earlier figures for May and June were revised down substantially...But it does not appear that the slowdown in job growth has opened up a large margin of slack in the labor market—an outcome we want to avoid. The unemployment rate, while edging up in July, stands at a historically low level of 4.2 percent and has been broadly stable over the past year. Other indicators of labor market conditions are also little changed or have softened only modestly, including quits, layoffs, the ratio of vacancies to unemployment, and nominal wage growth. Labor supply has softened in line with demand, sharply lowering the "breakeven" rate of job creation needed to hold the unemployment rate constant. Indeed, labor force growth has slowed considerably this year with the sharp falloff in immigration, and the labor force participation rate has edged down in recent months.

Overall, while the labor market appears to be in balance, it is a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers. This unusual situation suggests that downside risks to employment are rising. And if those risks materialize, they can do so quickly in the form of sharply higher layoffs and rising unemployment.

I've been signaling that the labor market will be what goes first if the economy starts to hit the hard landing that some pundits have been ensuring me will happen, but I agree with Powell's assessment that the labor market is in a "curious" state of balance.

While the July jobs report that Powell mentions was a huge sign of weakness, he's also right that the report left out the context that both demand and supply are lowering together. That doesn't magically fix the issue of the deep revisions, but it does put it into some perspective.

Powell's mention of GDP was very brief, but there is little to talk about, since its growth was more largely attributed to AI CapEx than to consumption.

At the same time, GDP growth has slowed notably in the first half of this year to a pace of 1.2 percent, roughly half the 2.5 percent pace in 2024. The decline in growth has largely reflected a slowdown in consumer spending. As with the labor market, some of the slowing in GDP likely reflects slower growth of supply or potential output.

The decline in GDP growth rate can also be attributed to importer/exporter behavior changes due to tariff policy uncertainty, where front-loading (buying ahead of time to create artificial surpluses) took place in Q4 of last year and Q1 of this year, with reduced imports in Q2 as businesses don't need to replenish inventory at the usual rate, since many of them have these man-made surpluses to deal with still.

Inflation was the big ticket item, so Powell made sure to get to it last of the three big indicators. Here's what he had to say about the currently sticky inflation rate:

Turning to inflation, higher tariffs have begun to push up prices in some categories of goods. Estimates based on the latest available data indicate that total PCE prices rose 2.6 percent over the 12 months ending in July. Excluding the volatile food and energy categories, core PCE prices rose 2.9 percent, above their level a year ago. Within core, prices of goods increased 1.1 percent over the past 12 months, a notable shift from the modest decline seen over the course of 2024. In contrast, housing services inflation remains on a downward trend, and nonhousing services inflation is still running at a level a bit above what has been historically consistent with 2 percent inflation...

The effects of tariffs on consumer prices are now clearly visible. We expect those effects to accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts. The question that matters for monetary policy is whether these price increases are likely to materially raise the risk of an ongoing inflation problem. A reasonable base case is that the effects will be relatively short lived—a one-time shift in the price level. Of course, "one-time" does not mean "all at once." It will continue to take time for tariff increases to work their way through supply chains and distribution networks. Moreover, tariff rates continue to evolve, potentially prolonging the adjustment process.

It is also possible, however, that the upward pressure on prices from tariffs could spur a more lasting inflation dynamic, and that is a risk to be assessed and managed. One possibility is that workers, who see their real incomes decline because of higher prices, demand and get higher wages from employers, setting off adverse wage–price dynamics. Given that the labor market is not particularly tight and faces increasing downside risks, that outcome does not seem likely.

Another possibility is that inflation expectations could move up, dragging actual inflation with them. Inflation has been above our target for more than four years and remains a prominent concern for households and businesses. Measures of longer-term inflation expectations, however, as reflected in market- and survey-based measures, appear to remain well anchored and consistent with our longer-run inflation objective of 2 percent.

Of course, we cannot take the stability of inflation expectations for granted. Come what may, we will not allow a one-time increase in the price level to become an ongoing inflation problem.

I was surprised at how dovish Powell was on the effects of tariffs, but was pleased with his comments about "one time" and "all at once." As we're seeing the tariff situation play out, one of these structural changes mentioned earlier, it's clear that the effects will not be all at once. It's also clear that the outcome will be multi-faceted, with some hit to consumer prices, some hit to producer prices, and hits to margins across the supply chain. This makes it difficult for the Fed to ascertain what pricing movements are related to tariffs and what are related to excess liquidity creating outsized demand.

I've been saying that even though the Fed is in an economic "rock and hard place," they will cut at the September FOMC meeting. Powell all but confirms this with his conclusion for the outlook part of his speech.

Putting the pieces together, what are the implications for monetary policy? In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate. Our policy rate is now 100 basis points closer to neutral than it was a year ago, and the stability of the unemployment rate and other labor market measures allows us to proceed carefully as we consider changes to our policy stance. Nonetheless, with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.

Monetary policy is not on a preset course. FOMC members will make these decisions, based solely on their assessment of the data and its implications for the economic outlook and the balance of risks. We will never deviate from that approach.

That is as close to, "I'm leaning toward a rate cut," as we are likely to get out of Powell. Insistence that they will remain data-dependent is a cover, but it only covers so much of their intentions here. It's clear that more rate cuts will be necessary to keep the labor market healthy, and I maintain that we are most likely to get a 25bp cut in September and another in October, before we go back into a "wait and see" period of holding rates for a few meetings.

The market seems to agree that we'll get a rate cut in September, now more than it has in the past day, week, or month.

Of course, new data that materially skews the trajectory of inflation or unemployment could cause course correction.

Monetary Policy Framework

I won't get too into this section of the speech, mostly because I find it inconsequential to the immediate decisions on monetary policy, but it should be of note that Powell said these lines during his discussion on how the framework the Fed uses is changing:

In particular, we said that, following periods when inflation had been running persistently below 2 percent, appropriate monetary policy would likely aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time...

We cannot say for certain where rates will settle out over the longer run, but their neutral level may now be higher than during the 2010s, reflecting changes in productivity, demographics, fiscal policy, and other factors that affect the balance between saving and investment...

We also continue to view a longer-run inflation rate of 2 percent as most consistent with our dual-mandate goals. We believe that our commitment to this target is a key factor helping keep longer-term inflation expectations well anchored. Experience has shown that 2 percent inflation is low enough to ensure that inflation is not a concern in household and business decision-making while also providing a central bank with some policy flexibility to provide accommodation during economic downturns.

All of this was essentially to say that the Fed used to view too low inflation as a reason to allow some inflation later, so that a 2% average could be reached, but that this is no longer the case. In fact, Powell said that they may see a neutral rate higher than 2% may be necessary to keep inflation down, now that we are no longer in the era of super low inflation following the housing bubble.

What Powell Didn't Say

There are structural changes coming to the Fed, as President Trump continues his attacks on Powell and the Fed more broadly, with no sign of slowing down.

At the next FOMC meeting, we'll likely have Stephen Miran there to push rate cuts on the committee, something he has pledged to do. He has been dubious about the jobs data (0:10), and so may threaten some of that "data dependence," the current FOMC prides itself on.

Now the DOJ is investigating Fed governor Cook, which may end up shoring more seats for folks like Miran to hold temporarily, and eventually for appointees to fill. I expect the people chosen for these positions, like Miran, to be aligned with President Trump's vision of immediate and steep rate cuts, and those views will flow into the FOMC as governors are replaced and empty seats filled.

These changes may spur on rate cuts sooner rather than later, especially come May of next year when Powell's term ends and a new Fed chair will be appointed.

I expect voting in the Senate confirmations to go along party lines.

Until then, expect the Fed to move at its current snail's pace, with a few small cuts over the next few months and then a period of "wait and see."

What will be interesting to see is if long bond rates come down alongside short rates; during the hawkish cuts we saw last year, long treasury rates rose in defiance of the Fed.

Conclusion

Ultimately, Fed chair Powell's Jackson Hole speech was a boon for markets because it demonstrated that the Fed is watching the economy closely and that they are starting to turn dovish on rates again, signaling coming rate cuts this year.

I still expect two cuts, with the first in September at the next FOMC meeting. Now, so does the market. If we get that cut, I expect the market to react positively. These are necessary for the Fed to reach its "soft landing." It may take time for rates to be fully wound down, and we may see a higher neutral rate than initially expected, closer to 3% over the long-run.

If you were following the Dot Plot, you should've already known that, but hearing it from Powell is a confidence boost.

Turning to inflation, higher tariffs have begun to push up prices in some categories of goods. Estimates based on the latest available data indicate that total PCE prices rose 2.6 percent over the 12 months ending in July. Excluding the volatile food and energy categories, core PCE prices rose 2.9 percent, above their level a year ago. Within core, prices of goods increased 1.1 percent over the past 12 months, a notable shift from the modest decline seen over the course of 2024. In contrast, housing services inflation remains on a downward trend, and nonhousing services inflation is still running at a level a bit above what has been historically consistent with 2 percent inflation (figure 4).4

The effects of tariffs on consumer prices are now clearly visible. We expect those effects to accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts. The question that matters for monetary policy is whether these price increases are likely to materially raise the risk of an ongoing inflation problem. A reasonable base case is that the effects will be relatively short lived—a one-time shift in the price level. Of course, "one-time" does not mean "all at once." It will continue to take time for tariff increases to work their way through supply chains and distribution networks. Moreover, tariff rates continue to evolve, potentially prolonging the adjustment process.

It is also possible, however, that the upward pressure on prices from tariffs could spur a more lasting inflation dynamic, and that is a risk to be assessed and managed. One possibility is that workers, who see their real incomes decline because of higher prices, demand and get higher wages from employers, setting off adverse wage–price dynamics. Given that the labor market is not particularly tight and faces increasing downside risks, that outcome does not seem likely.

Another possibility is that inflation expectations could move up, dragging actual inflation with them. Inflation has been above our target for more than four years and remains a prominent concern for households and businesses. Measures of longer-term inflation expectations, however, as reflected in market- and survey-based measures, appear to remain well anchored and consistent with our longer-run inflation objective of 2 percent.

data.title: Equinox Gold: On Track For A Million Ounces, Supported By Gold Prices (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Buy Rating For Equinox Gold Corp.

This analysis believes that it is not unreasonable to buy the NYSE-listed shares of Equinox Gold Corp. (NYSE:EQX), upgrading from the “Hold” rating in our previous analysis.

In its financial and operating results for the second quarter of 2025, Equinox Gold describes itself under the “ABOUT EQUINOX GOLD” section as follows:

Equinox Gold is a Canadian mining company positioned for growth with a strong foundation of high-quality, long-life gold operations in Canada and across the Americas, and a pipeline of development and expansion projects.

The Company's Portfolio Consists Of The Following Assets

Canada

The Greenstone Mine is an active open-pit gold mine located 4 km south of Geraldton, Ontario, Canada. Equinox Gold Corp. is the 100% owner of the Greenstone Mine.

The United States of America (former Calibre Mining assets)

The Mesquite Mine is an active open-pit gold mine located 50 km northwest of Yuma, California. Equinox Gold Corp. is the 100% owner of the Mesquite Mine.

The Pan Mine (sold to Minera Alamos Inc.) is an active open-pit gold mine located 74 km west of Ely, Nevada, United States. Equinox Gold Corp. owns the Pan Mine entirely. On August 7, 2025, Equinox Gold agreed to sell its 100% interest in the Pan Mine and other minor assets in Nevada, including the Gold Rock Project and the Illipah Project, to Minera Alamos Inc. (TSXV: MAI) for US$115 million. The Pan Mine is seen as a non-core asset in the portfolio of Equinox Gold Corp., and effectively, it is the smaller contributor to Equinox Gold's total consolidated gold production. Equinox Gold will receive US$90 million in cash and US$25 million in Minera Alamos common shares, up to 9.99% of the issued and outstanding shares of Minera Alamos. The transaction should close in the fourth quarter of 2025.

Darren Hall, CEO of Equinox Gold, commented:

“The sale of our non-core Nevada Assets reflects our commitment to portfolio optimization and disciplined capital allocation. This transaction simplifies our business and allows the team to focus our efforts and capital on core operations and key development opportunities, positioning Equinox Gold to drive greater shareholder returns. The US$90 million in cash proceeds will strengthen our balance sheet, and the significant equity ownership will provide continued exposure to the upside within the Nevada Assets as well as to Minera Alamos’ existing high-quality portfolio.”

Nicaragua (former Calibre Mining assets)

La Libertad Complex is an active open-pit mine and underground gold and silver mining operation located 4 km northwest of La Libertad, Nicaragua. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the La Libertad Complex.

The La El Limon Complex is an active open-pit and underground gold and silver mine located 38 km northeast of León, Nicaragua. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the El Limon Complex.

Brazil

The Aurizona mine is an active open-pit gold mine located 13 km north of Godofredo Viana, Brazil. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the Aurizona mine.

The Fazenda Brasileiro mine is an active open-pit and underground gold mine located 11 km east of Salgadalia, Brazil. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the Fazenda Brasileiro mine.

The Santa Luz mine is an active open-pit gold mine located 28 km north of Santa Luz, Brazil. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the Santa Luz mine.

RDM Gold Mine is an active open-pit gold mine located 560 km north of the Brazilian capital, Belo Horizonte. Equinox Gold Corp. owns 100% of the RDM Gold Mine.

The following screenshot shows the production and cost situation of Equinox Gold's asset portfolio on a consolidated basis and by mine:

Main Growth Projects

The Valentine Gold Project (former Calibre Mining assets) is located 86 km southeast of Corner Brook, Newfoundland and Labrador, Canada. Equinox Gold owns 100% of the Valentine Gold Project.

As a diversified mineral exploration company in central Newfoundland, directly adjacent to the Valentine Gold Project, Canterra Minerals Corporation (OTCQB: CTMCF) (CTM:CA) has something to say about Equinox Gold's Valentine Gold Project. In its exploration report dated June 16, 2025, it states:

Canterra's strategic land position in Central Newfoundland has become increasingly valuable. Adjacent to Canterra's Wilding Project, Equinox Gold is advancing construction of the Valentine Gold Mine, following its acquisition of Calibre Mining ( CXB:CA). The mine is expected to become Newfoundland's largest gold mine and one of Canada's top gold producers.

And

“Each target demonstrates key geological characteristics analogous to those at the Valentine Mine, a 4.0 Moz Au M&I and 1.1 Moz Au Inferred resource (2024) 1.”

The updated 2022 Valentine Mine Feasibility Study envisions an open pit and conventional milling operation with an initial mine life of 14 years and average annual gold production of 195,000 ounces over the first 12 years at an estimated AISC of US$1,007 per ounce.

First gold production is expected towards the end of the third quarter of 2025.

The Castle Mountain Mine is located 100 km south of Las Vegas, California, United States. Equinox Gold indirectly owns 100% of the Castle Mountain Mine.

In its August 11, 2025, announcement that its Castle Mountain Mine Phase 2 project had been accepted into the United States Federal Permitting Improvement Steering Council's FAST-41 program, Equinox Gold stated:

Equinox Gold acquired Castle Mountain in December 2017 and operated a small open-pit heap leach gold mine from Q4 2020 to Q3 2024. The mine is currently performing re-leaching procedures while the Company advances permitting, engineering and optimization work.

In the same announcement, Darren Hall, CEO of Equinox Gold, said:

"Castle Mountain is a high-quality growth opportunity for Equinox Gold in a tier-one jurisdiction. Based on the March 2021 Feasibility Study, the project is expected to produce approximately 200,000 ounces of gold annually over a 14-year mine life, totaling 3.2 million ounces. With FAST-41 permitting status in place we've initiated study updates and project optimization to align with the permitting timeline and position the project for a timely construction decision. Once in production Castle Mountain will be a cornerstone asset, reinforcing our strategy of building a portfolio of long-life, low-cost mines in top-tier jurisdictions."

On its website, Equinox Gold states that

Permitting and front-end engineering are advancing for Castle Mountain Phase 2, with permits expected around mid-2027

The Los Filos Complex is located 10 km southwest of Mezcala, Guerrero, Mexico. Equinox Gold indirectly owns 100% of the Los Filos Complex.

In its April 1, 2025, announcement regarding the indefinite suspension of operations at the Los Filos mine due to the expiration of the land access agreement with the municipality of Carrizalillo on March 31, 2025, Equinox Gold stated:

Long-term agreements with all three local communities are essential to provide the economic and investment conditions necessary for continued operations at the Los Filos Mine, including the proposed construction of a new 10,000 tonnes-per-day carbon-in-leach processing plant to increase gold recoveries from higher-grade ore.

Regarding the Los Filos expansion, Equinox Gold's website refers to an updated feasibility study dated October 2022. This study forecasts 280,000 ounces of gold annually at all-in costs of US$1,081 per ounce (using 2022 input prices) over an (extended) life of mine (LOM) of 14.5 years. This is based on the continued development of the Bermejal underground deposit and the construction of a 10,000 tonne per day carbon-in-leach (CIL) processing facility.

On Los Filos, Equinox Gold’s CEO said:

“The feasibility study confirms a path to expand Los Filos to a large-scale, long-life gold mine with a substantial mineral reserve and resource endowment and opportunity for further growth. […] Any decision to proceed with the Los Filos expansion will be made considering the operating stability in the region, market conditions and availability and cost of capital.”

The Reason Behind The Rating Upgrade

We are raising our rating from “Hold” to “Buy” as NYSE-listed EQX stock can now benefit from the synergies of the merger with Calibre Mining Corp. Calibre Mining Corp., a former gold and silver mining and exploration company headquartered in Vancouver, Canada, with operations in North America and Nicaragua, ceased to exist following its merger with EQX on June 20, 2025. These benefits include economies of scale, as the company can now target production of 950,000 ounces per year. This results in more balanced geographical risk across a larger asset portfolio, as Nicaragua’s high risk is further mitigated by Brazil’s moderate risk and the U.S./Canada’s low risk, as well as overall lower operating costs. Added to this is the gold price, which remains robust thanks to continued strong tailwinds following double-digit growth this year. The combination of these factors provides solid upside for earnings and cash flow. Rising profitability and cash flow typically create positive market sentiment, as these two metrics are linked to the possibility that the company is creating value and returning it to the market through share buyback programs or dividend payments one day. Positive sentiment on the NYSE surrounding EQX is what EQX shareholders need if they have aspirations of being rewarded with the appreciation of market value. EQX shareholders, therefore, appear to be very well positioned to achieve their goal.

Equinox Gold Corp. Performance Since Our Last Article

Since our last analysis on March 12, 2025, shares of NYSE-listed Equinox Gold Corp. (symbol “EQX”) have risen by +19.34% and outperformed the S&P 500 by +13.77%. These changes were in effect at the time of writing. As expected, the gain was driven by optimistic sentiment around the benefits of merging with another gold operator showing signs of sustainable growth, and together creating a large gold producer targeting annual production of 950,000 ounces, amid a strong gold price. What is achieved through a merger is not the simple arithmetic sum of units, but rather positive synergies that would otherwise be unattainable with standalone companies. Ultimately, we highlighted the positive synergies resulting from becoming a major gold producer, on track to produce one million ounces of gold per year. Since our last analysis, this transformation is seen as driven by well-performing mines and promising growth projects, which could bring even more ounces while on the way to a major producer, as well as cost savings from a substantially higher production profile. The gold price continued to benefit from robust demand as the global situation was so uncertain that investors' recourse to safe-haven assets strengthened (and gold is the ultimate safe-haven investment). Hopes of an interest rate cut also led investors to favour the yellow metal, as gold, unlike US Treasury bonds, does not yield interest income. The only gain from owning gold comes from the appreciation of the troy ounces in the market.

Solid Results In The Second Quarter Of 2025 And Ready For A Major Inflection In The Third Quarter Of 2025

The following screenshot from Equinox Gold Corp.'s operating and financial results report for the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025 illustrates the performance of the entire mining portfolio, which is now owned by Equinox Gold Corp. following the merger.

The operating performance of the portfolio in the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025:

  • In the second quarter of 2025, the entire portfolio produced 219,122 ounces of gold at a cash cost per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) of US$1,478 and an AISC per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) of US$1,959.
  • In the first half of 2025, the entire portfolio produced 401,211 ounces of gold at a cash cost per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) of US$1,548 and an AISC per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) of US$1,968.

We have considered the cost items “Cash costs per ounce sold - excluding Los Filos” and “AISC per ounce sold - excluding Los Filos”. These are consolidated and therefore take into account the overall picture of all assets operated by the merged company, Equinox Gold Corp. They provide a clearer picture of whether the entire mine portfolio is on track compared to the full-year 2025 guidance, particularly from a cost perspective. The full-year 2025 guidance excludes the Los Filos mine complex, which was shut down indefinitely on April 1, 2025, following the expiration of the land access agreement with the Carrizalillo community on March 31, 2025. As previously discussed in this analysis, the October 2022 feasibility study anticipates an extension of mine life and an increase in annual production through the potential expansion of Los Filos. But the latter is on hold until Equinox Gold can enter into new long-term agreements with all its community partners.

Commenting on the second quarter of 2025 financial and operating results, Darren Hall, CEO of Equinox Gold, said:

“Equinox Gold is entering a pivotal growth phase. Q2 delivered solid results, led by Greenstone, where mining rates increased 23% and processing rates improved 20% over Q1. Building on that momentum, Q3 is off to a strong start, with quarter-to-date ex-pit mining volumes 10% higher than Q2 and process plant throughput averaging 24.5 kptd over the last 30 days, including more than one-third of the days above nameplate capacity of 27 ktpd. This sets the stage for our true inflection point in Q3, driven by a full-quarter contribution from the Calibre assets, first ore processed at Valentine, and continued improvement at Greenstone. […]”

As Equinox Gold Corp.'s combined mining portfolio reaches a turning point this quarter, the following applies:

  • Consolidated gold production could well range between the mid-point of 850,000 ounces and the high end of the full-year 2025 guidance.
  • Given the positive trends observed in cash cost per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) and AISC per ounce sold (excluding Los Filos) in Q2-2025 on Q2-2024, H1-2025 on H1-2024 and Q2-2025 on Q1-2025, Equinox Gold Corp.'s 2025 costs could be towards the lower limit and mid-range of their respective guidance.

The gold price has performed very well. The company reported an “average realized gold price” of $3,207/ounce for the quarter ending June 30, 2025, representing an increase of 12.2% quarter-on-quarter and 37.8% year-on-year.

For the first half of 2025, the “average realised gold price” was $3,033/ounce, representing an increase of 38.1% year-on-year.

Thanks to robust operating results, boosted impressively by the bullish gold price, Equinox Gold Corp. was able to achieve higher profitability.

Seeking Alpha reported on August 14, 2025, “ Equinox Gold beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; […]” highlighting the following figures:

 Equinox Gold press release (NYSE: EQX): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.11 beats by $0.09.

 Revenue of $478.6M (+77.7% Y/Y) beats by $8.6M.

[…]

 Cash flow from operations before changes in non-cash working capital of $126.0 million ($132.9 million after changes in non-cash working capital).

 Mine-site free cash flow before changes in non-cash working capital of $154.5 million ($178.4 million after changes in non-cash working capital).

 Adjusted EBITDA of $200.5 million.

The reader can welcome the year-on-year improvement with the pricing in of the merger and the bullish momentum of the gold price:

  • Cash flow from operating activities before changes in non-cash working capital of $126 million increased 217.4%, or 3.17 times, year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $200.5 million increased 344.6%, or 4.45 times, compared to the previous year.
  • The adjusted EBITDA margin was 41.9% of total revenue for the second quarter of 2025, an increase of 2,520 basis points compared to the adjusted EBITDA margin of 16.7% of total revenue for the second quarter of 2024.
  • Sustaining expenditures of $71.1 million and non-sustaining expenditures of $42.3 million totalled $113.4 million. Free cash flow (= operating cash flow less total capital expenditures) was approximately $83.7 million.

The Balance Sheet

The balance sheet showed unrestricted cash and equivalents of $406.7 million, burdened by net debt of $1,373.7 million as of June 30, 2025, meaning Equinox Gold Corp. had total debt of approximately $1,780.4 million.

  • Seeking Alpha shows that Equinox Gold Corp. generated operating income of $103.8 million in the second quarter of 2025, an increase of 1,659.37% year-over-year. The merger also increased interest expense on $1,780.4 million total debt to $45.3 million in the second quarter of 2025, or 119.32% year-over-year. Operating income is expected to improve, supported by rising gold ounces, improved operating costs, and a strong gold price. However, let's assume an operating income of $415.2 million for the last 12 months. As loans and borrowings are subject to fixed interest rates, regarding interest expense on a 12-month basis, we can more confidently assume it could be approximately $181.2 million. The 12-month interest coverage ratio is therefore estimated at $415.2 million divided by an estimated $181.2 million, leading to a reading of 2.3x. Assuming this ratio improves due to the operating and pricing factors already mentioned, a ratio of 2.3x already signals that Equinox Gold Corp. can easily borrow and will easily meet its financial obligations, including interest payments. Investors typically expect a ratio between 1.5x and 2.0x (the minimum acceptable level). A company below this ratio is considered struggling to repay debt and interest.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $200.5 million in the second quarter of 2025. Now consider the following factors: A) expected higher sales volumes due to increased gold production as a result of the merger, B) robust gold prices per ounce, and C) continued positive trends in operating costs. We expect trailing-12-month adjusted EBITDA of $802 million, although this could be significantly higher due to tailwinds from operations and the gold price. We believe it is not too bold to assume an annual adjusted EBITDA of at least $802 million. Now we can calculate the next leverage ratio as net debt of $1,373.7 million to annual adjusted EBITDA of $802 million. This ratio is 1.7 and suggests that Equinox Gold Corp. can repay its debt with its earnings in less than two years. It could be better, as the rule of thumb in the gold mining and exploration industry is to maintain a healthy net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio between 1.0x and 1.5x. Equinox Gold is not far from joining the ranks of virtuous gold stocks in terms of its net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio. While the path to approaching 0.5x, the ratio typically enjoyed by more established, producing gold mining companies, is somewhat longer. However, it is not an impossible milestone to achieve, as long as the gold price remains as stable as the scenario suggests and Equinox Gold Corp. continues to improve its gold production and costs.
  • The company has total liquidity of $406.7 million and potential cash flow from operations of $525 million to $545 million on a 12-month basis (=$132.9 million in Q2 2025 after changes in non-cash working capital x 4 quarters). Equinox Gold Corp. will also receive $90 million in cash from the sale of the Pan Mine and other smaller assets in Nevada, including the Gold Rock Project and the Illipah Project, earlier this month to Minera Alamos Inc. (TSXV: MAI) for a total of $115 million of equity in Minera Alamos. This compares with estimated total capital expenditures (growth capital + exploration) of $260 million to $310 million based on the pro forma consolidated Equinox Gold guidance for the full year 2025 (including Calibre assets from January 1, 2025), plus an additional approximately $285 million (=$71.1 million in Q2 2025 x 4 quarters) in sustaining expenditures to maintain current production levels and existing operations.
  • Seeking Alpha shows that Equinox Gold Corp. has an Altman Z-score of 0.72 (this metric is available here under the “Risk” section). This implies an uncertain financial situation and the risk of insolvency within a few years. But, as we have seen, Equinox Gold Corp. has the current and future liquidity to continue its global activities as a gold mining and exploration company and pursue its growth goals without further straining the balance sheet. Also, assuming that there are no problems with the mines, but that they continue to meet the company's goal of one million ounces per year at increasingly manageable costs, and that the gold price remains stable, we believe that the Altman Z-score can improve and move out of financial distress territory, and this evolution would obviously have a very positive impact on Equinox Gold Corp.'s stock market.

Outlook: Higher Ounces Will Meet Robust Gold Prices

Commenting on Equinox Gold Corp.'s release of its financial and operating results for Q2 2025, Darren Hall, CEO of Equinox Gold, also said:

“We expect a strong second half of the year, with production on track to meet our full-year consolidated guidance of 785,000 to 915,000 ounces and anticipate continued growth in both production and cash flow into 2026.

“Our focus is clear as we grow into a top-tier producer – operational excellence, disciplined capital allocation, and deliver on our commitments to drive debt reduction, optimize our balance sheet, and maximize returns for shareholders.”

In terms of gold prices, the benchmark Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) was trading at $3,373.03/oz at the time of writing, up +28.53% YTD and +35.60% over the past 12 months, driven by safe-haven demand and hopes for interest rate cuts.

Seeking Alpha recently reported parts of Barrick Gold CEO Mark Bristow's comments during a CNBC interview, writing that:

Barrick Gold CEO Mark Bristow expects the gold price (XAUUSD:CUR) to remain strong with “more risk on the upside than the downside,” despite uncertainty around potential U.S. tariffs on gold imports.

[…]

He attributed the strong gold performance to “deglobalization and all the other challenges within the global economy,” suggesting these fundamental factors continue to support bullish sentiment.

WisdomTree analysts, as published on Kitco, describe two scenarios for the future of gold prices. They predict a bullish scenario, where upward factors dominate, but they also predict a bearish scenario, where downward factors dominate. The average of the two gold price forecasts is $3,587.5 per ounce, which is assumed to be the price target in 12 months. From current levels, the price of gold would need to increase by approximately 6.4% to reach an average price of $3,587.5 per ounce in 12 months.

Market Valuation Of The Stock: The Share Price Is High, But The Outlook Is Interesting. Buying On Dips Is Always A Better Strategy

NYSE-listed shares of Equinox Gold Corp., under the symbol EQX, were trading at $8.08 per share at the time of this article, representing a market capitalisation of $5.88 billion.

The shares are trading above $6.555 per share middle point of the 52-week range of $4.95 to $8.16 per share, and are well above the MA Ribbon. The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 73.66x also suggests that the shares are close to overbought and that significant, albeit temporary, downside potential is gaining momentum. So, potential buyers are better off for the time being waiting for a sharp pullback in the share price. The stock market of Equinox Gold Corp. is not illiquid, as the average volume (3 months) is 14.56 million NYSE-listed shares traded on a daily basis. But buying activity on significantly lower share price levels is always a preferred strategy to mitigate uncertainty in the financial markets, especially with gold and other commodities.

A cooling in the gold price could trigger the same for NYSE-listed Equinox Gold Corp. shares, and fundamentally, we see two things that could currently create headwinds for the yellow metal: A) Inflation fears forcing the Federal Reserve to return to a cautious stance on interest rate cuts after the almost certain rate cut in September, as gold as a zero-yielding asset will suffer in the climate of interest rates that tend to remain elevated for longer than initially assumed. B) A ceasefire in Ukraine: If the US and the Club of Willing European Countries (which may send troops to Ukraine) supporting Ukraine on the one hand manage to find an agreement to end hostilities with Russia on the other, it will dampen gold's appeal as a safe haven, and a price that develops less robustly than before for a while could also affect Equinox Gold Corp. shares.

Conclusion

This analysis thinks that it is reasonable if people want to buy NYSE-listed shares of Equinox Gold Corp. under the symbol EQX, upgraded from the “Hold” rating in our previous analysis, as all the caveats seem to be gradually disappearing. Following the agreement with Calibre Mining, Equinox Gold's overall business is positioned for growth with its long-lived, high-grade gold operations across the Americas, as well as a pipeline of development and expansion projects. The financial and operating results for the second quarter of 2025 and the first half of 2025 already provide hints of the growth that the stock market can hardly wait to see continuing to materialise. The increasing gold production, also supported by strong growth projects, is expected to reach one million ounces of gold in the near future, and costs are becoming more and more manageable. The gold price will remain robust, and the company can be reasonably optimistic about its earnings and cash flow outlook. The balance sheet has more than sufficient monetary resources to support the business and finance the growth. The shares are currently trading high, and we believe that it is always better to buy them at falling prices to mitigate the uncertainty in the financial markets, which are now more unstable than ever due to the turbulent global environment and trade war with increased tariffs.

data.title: Platform Consolidation Pair Trade: Long Microsoft, Short Asana
data.description:
A few years ago, when mobility restrictions led to unprecedented growth of point solutions for businesses, Asana, Inc. (NYSE:ASAN) emerged as a go-to project management solutions provider for millions of users. After trading sideways around $25, ASAN stock jumped close to $150 in late 2021 as its revenue almost tripled between January 2020 and January 2022. The reality check came shortly when zero interest rates quickly became a distant reality amid surging inflation. Asana has lost more than 90% of its market value from the peak stock prices registered in 2021.

Today, with the rise of AI, I believe the playing field for software solutions providers is changing dramatically. The odds are unmistakably tilting in favor of hyperscale platform owners at the expense of niche players because these large-scale platform players posses several key ingredients to success, including AI dominance, access to massive datasets, unmatched infrastructure for both model training and inference, and also highly effective distribution channels that enable the deployment of AI-powered software tools at almost no marginal cost.

Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ:MSFT), aided by the rise of its Copilot offerings, is increasingly turning standalone software categories into mere features of its broad platform. This poses an existential threat to niche, single-product software companies. Asana, unfortunately, is in the crosshairs. After a careful analysis of the financial position of both these companies, I believe there is a strong case for a pair trade by going long Microsoft stock and selling Asana stock. Before we delve deeper into the thesis for this pair trade, let me remind investors that I hardly short stocks and I will not be shorting Asana. However, I will present clear evidence to support this pair trade, whether or not it involves shorting Asana or simply selling Asana stock to load on MSFT.

The Long Case For Microsoft

Microsoft is a leader in the global AI race, and this is a well-established fact. I am not going to discuss its AI capabilities in detail here, but will present certain data points that highlight its growing influence on the work management sector.

First, I believe Microsoft 365 enjoys a massive distribution advantage compared to Asana. More than the product itself, I believe this distribution engine poses a major threat to Asana as Microsoft can literally place its work management tools directly into the existing workflows of its paying subscribers at no extra cost. There is strong evidence that SMBs are being captured by this unmatched distribution engine. For example, in Q4 FY2025 ended on June 30, Microsoft 265 Commercial seat count grew 6% YoY, driven by the growing adoption of its solutions by SMBs. For Asana, this is a direct threat as SMBs account for its primary target market. As a small business owner myself, Microsoft 365's appeal is straightforward. Rather than subscribing to a standalone work management tool, I prefer paying a single subscription fee to access Office, Teams, Copilot, Security, Planner, and work management tools that are embedded into Teams, Outlook, and Office.

Exhibit 1: Productivity and Business Processes performance

Second, Microsoft's AI automation efforts threaten to disrupt the work management industry, posing a major risk to Asana. As a Microsoft 365 user, I have first-hand experience in how Copilot can be used to simplify seemingly complex tasks. When Copilot was first introduced, it was thought of as an AI feature to write/summarize emails but the tool has grown into an automation powerhouse. You don't have to look beyond Satya Nadella's recent remarks to understand the deeply integrated automated workflow solutions Microsoft now offers. As explained by him, a user can now discuss a project in a Teams chat, have Copilot summarize the action items, turn those items into tasks in Planner, assign them, and track progress, all without ever leaving the Teams interface.

Microsoft is aggressively investing in agentic AI features as well. If you are a Teams user, you may have noticed a couple of interesting agentic AI tools, such as Facilitator and Interpreter, that focus on managing meeting notes and translations, respectively.

Third, the growing developer and partner ecosystem around Microsoft's platform poses a threat to Asana's fundamental business model that promises to fix the "broken way" we work. The below visualization highlights Asana's core selling point.

Exhibit 2: Asana's value proposition

Microsoft is actively partnering with hundreds of software partners allowing them to integrate their products/tools with Copilot. Some of these partners include Adobe (ADBE), SAP (SAP), ServiceNow (NOW), and Workday (WDAY). As a result of these partnerships, Microsoft 365 users can now pull data from these platforms directly to Copilot and even Teams, removing a major barrier to seamless integration.

What's more, we can build our own agents within the Microsoft ecosystem too. According to company filings, in the past fiscal year, more than 3 million such agents were created using SharePoint and Copilot Studio. I believe this capability is a major threat to Asana.

In summary, I believe Microsoft's focus on workflow automation, growing its partner ecosystem, and its massive distribution network pose an existential threat to Asana in the long term. These strategies are already working. The last reporting quarter was the best in history for Microsoft 365 Copilot, and some of the noteworthy customer additions/expansions include Barclays planning to roll out Microsoft 365 Copilot to 100,000 employees globally, UBS expanding Microsoft 365 Copilot to all of its employees, and Adobe, KPMG, Pfizer, and Wells Fargo buying more than 25,000 seats each during the quarter.

The Short: Asana's Financials Are Flashing Red Alerts

Asana's financials are already flashing red alerts, and the market has not been oblivious to these warning signs.

The company's growth has decelerated from high double-digits to single digits, reflecting the new fundamental reality facing the company and the work management software industry in a post-Covid environment. For FY2026, Asana has guided for revenue growth of between 7% and 9%, suggesting that this growth deceleration is not a temporary phenomenon.

Exhibit 3: Asana revenue growth

In an already challenged market where growth is hard to find by, Microsoft 365's progress is likely to be felt by Asana in the coming years as customers, in my opinion, will gravitate toward platform solutions despite Asana arguably offering a better UI interface and feel.

Dollar-based net retention rate of Asana confirms that the company is finding it difficult to retain customers as it did in the past when the industry was primed for growth. For context, a rate below 100% means that existing customers are spending lower compared to a comparable period. In Q1 FY2026, Asana's dollar-based net retention rate was 95%. Even for those categorized as core customers (annual spending of $5,000+), the net retention rate was 96%. Even if you single out the largest customers served by Asana (annual spending of $100,000+), the dollar-based net retention rate was still 95%, which clearly indicates that Asana's most important customers are spending less than they used to. Not a good sign for a software company with just one product that has yet to see GAAP profits consistently. Even the landmark deal announced by Asana last quarter, which is reportedly the largest deal in the company's history worth over $100 million, marks a downgrade on an ACV basis according to CFO Sonalee Parekh.

Looking at the recent management commentary is enough to understand the severe headwinds facing Asana. Commenting on the outlook for net retention rate in Q2, CFO Parekh said:

However, while our in-quarter NRR was stable for the third consecutive quarter, we expect net retention in Q2 to be pressured. This is due to a combination of continued downgrade pressure, particularly in our enterprise and middle-market segments and the technology vertical.

In the last earnings call, COO Anne Raimondi provided some color on the expected headwinds and said that budget pressures faced by Asana's customers, ongoing workforce reductions, and consolidation of tools under centralized IT management are some of the main reasons limiting Asana's growth. This is as direct an admission as you could imagine of how platform consolidation has become a major threat to Asana.

In summary, Asana's recent financial performance reveals its vulnerabilities at a time when platform solutions are taking the center stage. Combine this with the management commentary regarding ACV downgrades, consolidation pressure, and retention challenges, and you have a company that is likely to face severe headwinds in the future. Microsoft, as I discussed earlier, is at the other end where it is well-positioned to benefit from the very trends that are keeping Asana under pressure.

Counter Thesis Rests On The Success (or not) of Asana AI Studio

The pair trade of selling ASAN and buying MSFT could go wrong if Asana makes a U-turn and returns to double-digit growth. In my opinion, the only way that this could happen is if its bet on Asana AI Studio delivers better-than-expected results. The core strategy involves positioning Asana as an integral part of human and AI coordination.

Exhibit 4: Asana AI Studio's capabilities

There is some evidence of early momentum for AI Studio. For instance, in the first quarter of its availability, it surpassed $1 million in ARR. This is good news for Asana investors. What is even better is that early adopters are reporting noteworthy productivity gains. According to the company, a case study by Clear Channel Outdoor reveals that the company saw a 60% reduction in manual work and a 69% reduction in time per request after it shifted to AI Studio.

While the early numbers are promising, I believe the scale of AI Studio would prove to be immaterial to help Asana negate the threat posed by the rise of platforms. Given the small scale of AI Studio, we are likely to see strong double or even triple-digit growth in revenue from this segment in the next few quarters, but in the broad context, AI Studio represents just a drop in the bucket as Asana is a company closing in $800 million in revenue annually. While the pivot to a consumption-based pricing scheme is encouraging, even a small decline in the per-seat-based business would be felt by Asana to a greater degree.

Also, the AI Studio is not a comprehensive suite of products just yet, and the company will have to invest millions of dollars aggressively to position it as a standalone tool that could attract high-value customers.

Valuation Supports The Pair Trade

Microsoft, as a pioneer in the AI space and a profitable tech giant, is valued at premium multiples. However, I argue that this embedded premium is well-deserved. For comparison of the two businesses, I chose an EV/Sales vs growth framework. Microsoft is currently valued at a forward EV/S multiple of 11.80 whereas Asana is valued at a much more modest EV/S multiple of 3.9. On paper, Asana looks much cheaper. However, when you incorporate growth into the equation, the picture becomes clearer. In FY2025, Microsoft delivered 15% revenue growth on a $245 billion base. That's no small feat. However, much smaller Asana could only deliver an 11% revenue growth on a base of $652 million in its most recent fiscal year. The difference is telling. Although Microsoft is a much larger business with almost 300x the revenue of Asana, it is still growing faster, aided by secular trends that are working in favor of the tech giant. Asana, on the other hand, is projecting a further deceleration in revenue growth in the current financial year.

With time, I expect Asana's EV/S multiple to shrink further while Microsoft's valuation remains stable as it takes market share in the project management industry.

Asana's valuation becomes even more concerning when you apply the rule of 40. Based on the Q1 FY2026 revenue growth rate of just 9% and an adjusted free cash flow margin of 5%, we arrive at a Rule of 40 score of 14% for Asana. This is exceptionally weak, to say the least. On one hand, Asana is not growing fast enough to command a premium valuation. On the other hand, its free cash flows are meager to even consider the company a value play. In contrast, Microsoft is a cash flow behemoth (operating cash flow of ~$136 billion in FY2025 and free cash flow of ~$71 billion).

In summary, I believe Microsoft's premium valuation is reasonable given the durable double-digit growth it is experiencing, unparalleled scale, strong profitability, and massive free cash flows. Asana, on the other hand, faces a significant risk of a valuation contraction as its growth has decelerated to a level that does not warrant premium SaaS multiples. The business environment is only going to get more challenging for the company.

Takeaway

As AI disrupts the work management industry, I believe Microsoft, with its platform strategy, is well-positioned to assert its dominance in the next five years. As for Asana, the outlook is challenging, with its future hanging by the expected success of AI Studio which requires substantial capital investments today. I believe Microsoft's premium valuation is well-supported while Asana faces a significant risk of a valuation contraction as it struggles to grow while failing to see any improvement in dollar-based net retention rate. This creates an opportunity for a pair trade which involves going long MSFT and selling/shorting ASAN.
